title: Effect of Bougie Use on First-Attempt Success in Tracheal Intubations: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.

items.ai_reason: Title section identifies the report as 'SYSTEMATIC REVIEW/META-ANALYSIS'.
Background and Importance sections describe the rationale for evaluating bougie use in the context of existing knowledge and ongoing debate.
Goals of This Investigation explicitly states the objective to assess the association between bougie use and first-attempt success.
Eligibility Criteria section specifies inclusion/exclusion criteria including population, intervention, comparator, study designs, and language restrictions.
Lists databases (Ovid Cochrane Central, Embase, Medline, Scopus, Web of Science) with dates searched (October 5, 2022 and updated June 15, 2023) and reference list review.
Appendix E1 provides detailed search strategies, with key terms described in the methods.
States pairs of reviewers (R.V.H., N.F., L.O.J.S.) independently screened titles/abstracts and full texts, with disagreements resolved by consensus.
Describes standardized form, one investigator extracted data with second reviewer cross-checking, disagreements resolved by consensus, and data obtained from DEVICE trial authors via email.
Lists primary outcome (first-attempt success) and secondary outcomes (hypoxemia, postintubation arrest, intubation duration, injuries, esophageal intubations, sore throat) with definitions.
Lists variables extracted including setting, RSI use, laryngoscopy type, blades, intubator experience, age, sex, indications, and Cormack-Lehane grades.
Specifies Cochrane RoB 2 for RCTs and modified Newcastle-Ottawa for observational studies, performed by one investigator and cross-checked by methodologist.
Specifies risk ratios with 95% CIs as the effect measure.
Eligibility criteria and subgroup planning describe how studies were grouped for syntheses by setting, design, etc.
No description of methods to handle missing summary statistics or data conversions.
Mentions forest plots, funnel plot, and tabulation; visual inspection of forest plots described.
Describes random-effects DerSimonian-Laird model, I² for heterogeneity, and Review Manager 5.4.1 software.
Describes planned subgroup analyses by study design, risk of bias, setting, training, and intubation device.
Describes sensitivity analyses excluding outlier studies (eg, BOUGIE trial) to assess effect on heterogeneity.
States a funnel plot was used to assess potential publication bias for the primary outcome.
States GRADE was used to assess the certainty of evidence at the outcome level.
Reports 2,699 studies screened, 134 full-text reviewed, 18 included, with Figure 1 flowchart.
Does not cite specific studies that appeared eligible but were excluded with reasons.
Table 1 presents characteristics of each included study with citations.
Reports risk of bias assessments with details in Appendix E3, noting all observational studies high risk and RCTs with some concerns.
Forest plots (Figures 2-4) and Table 1 present individual study data with effect estimates and confidence intervals.
Subgroup analyses summarize characteristics and risk of bias of contributing studies in Table 2.
Presents pooled RRs with 95% CIs and I² values for all syntheses including primary and subgroup meta-analyses.
Subgroup analyses by design, setting, risk of bias, laryngoscopy type, and difficult airway presented in Table 2.
Reports sensitivity analysis excluding the BOUGIE trial yielding RR 1.12 with reduced heterogeneity (I²=6%).
Reports funnel plot asymmetry findings in Appendix E4 and notes certainty was not decreased for this domain.
Certainty of Evidence section presents GRADE assessments (low overall, moderate for Cormack-Lehane III/IV).
Discussion compares findings with previous systematic reviews by Sheu et al and Tollman et al.
Limitations section discusses risk of bias in included studies, heterogeneity, and lack of intubator experience reporting.
Limitations discuss inability to perform subgroup analysis by intubator experience due to lack of reporting.
Discussion suggests bougie-first strategy for clinical practice and recommends future studies focus on first-attempt success without complications.
States protocol was registered on PROSPERO (CRD42023403212).
Data sharing statement notes the study protocol can be provided on request; PROSPERO registration provides access.
States the analysis of difficult airway subgroup was planned post hoc, explaining the amendment to the protocol.
Funding and support section states 'This study received no funding.'
States 'The authors report no conflicts of interest.'
Data sharing statement indicates data, protocol, statistical analysis plan, and analytic code can be provided on request.
Title explicitly identifies the report as a 'Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis'.
States the aim to evaluate evidence on bougie use and first-attempt tracheal intubation success.
Specifies inclusion (adults, RCTs and comparative observational studies, bougie vs no bougie) and exclusion (manikin and cadaver studies).
Lists databases (Ovid Cochrane Central, Ovid Embase, Ovid Medline, Scopus, Web of Science) and search date (inception to June 2023).
Although GRADE was used to assess certainty of evidence, no specific risk of bias assessment tool/method is mentioned.
Describes random-effects meta-analysis and GRADE for certainty of evidence.
Reports 18 included studies (12 RCTs) with 9,151 patients.
Reports pooled RR 1.11 (95% CI 1.06-1.17) favoring bougie, with subgroup estimates and confidence intervals.
Notes that the certainty of evidence was low.
Concludes bougie should probably be used first and not as a rescue device in emergency intubations.
No funding source is mentioned in the abstract.
No registration name or number is provided in the abstract.

title: Efficacy of prescribed opioids for acute pain after being discharged from the emergency department: A systematic review and meta-analysis.

items.ai_reason: Title explicitly states 'A systematic review and meta-analysis'
Introduction discusses existing knowledge on opioid prescribing in ED, conflicting evidence, and rationale for synthesizing data on post-discharge opioid efficacy
Explicit objective stated: 'synthesize the evidence regarding the efficacy of prescribed opioids compared to nonopioid analgesics for acute pain relief in ED-discharged patients'
Inclusion/exclusion criteria specified (RCTs, ED-discharged, opioids vs nonopioids; excluding chronic pain, SUD, ED-only studies, etc.)
Databases (MEDLINE, EMBASE, CINAHL, PsycINFO, CENTRAL), registries, Google Scholar listed with search dates (inception to May 2022, updated January 2023)
Search strategy themes described and full strategy referenced in Supplement Methods S1
Two independent reviewers screened titles/abstracts and full texts with third reviewer resolving discrepancies; Covidence used
Two reviewers extracted data independently with conflicts resolved by consensus; authors contacted for missing data
Primary outcome (pain intensity/relief) and secondary outcomes (adverse events, rescue analgesics) explicitly defined; final single time point used
Variables extracted listed: sample size, age range, inclusion/exclusion, type of opioid/nonopioid, outcomes, results
EPOC risk-of-bias tool used, two independent reviewers, third reviewer for conflicts, nine domains specified
SMD for pain efficacy and odds ratios for adverse events specified
Describes that data were pooled if available for at least three studies; describes how studies handled (e.g., kept oxycodone vs placebo comparison only)
Describes converting percentage data to SMD using Comprehensive Meta-analysis software
Forest plots described using RevMan 5.4; tables used
Random-effect model with inverse variance weighting described; I², tau-square, Cochran's Q for heterogeneity; software (RevMan, CMA) specified
Subgroup/sensitivity analyses described (codeine vs other opioids, adults vs pediatric)
Multiple sensitivity analyses described (short-term follow-up, excluding high RoB, excluding placebo comparator)
Authors explicitly state no publication bias test (funnel plot) was performed due to small number of studies; no alternative method described
No mention of GRADE or other certainty of evidence assessment methods
PRISMA flowchart presented (Figure 1) with numbers from 5942 references to six included studies
Discusses excluded study using dextropropoxyphene with reason; describes handling of comparators in two studies
Table 1 presents characteristics of all six included studies with citations
Risk of bias presented (one high, five low); Supplement Figure S1 shows EPOC assessment
Table 2 presents results for each study including primary results, adverse events, and rescue analgesics
Discussion summarises characteristics and risk of bias among contributing studies for each synthesis
Forest plots present SMDs with 95% CIs and I² heterogeneity for both efficacy and adverse events
Subgroup results presented (codeine vs other opioids, adults vs pediatrics) with chi-square tests
Sensitivity analyses results presented (short-term follow-up, excluding high RoB study, excluding placebo)
No assessment of reporting bias presented; authors explicitly chose not to perform funnel plot analysis
No certainty of evidence assessment (e.g., GRADE) presented
Discussion compares findings with previous systematic reviews and contextualizes results
Limitations section discusses heterogeneity of populations, exclusion criteria, North American limitations of included evidence
Limitations of review process discussed (e.g., not assessing publication bias due to few studies, focus only on post-discharge)
Discusses implications for practice (prescribing strategies) and future research needs
PROSPERO registration number provided (CRD42022332009)
Protocol accessible via PROSPERO registration
No mention of amendments to the registered protocol
Funding source (Fonds des Urgentistes de l'Hôpital du Sacré-Coeur de Montréal) stated; role of funders described as none
Conflict of Interest Statement: 'The authors declare no competing interests'
No statement on availability of data collection forms, extracted data, analytic code, or other materials
Title explicitly states 'A systematic review and meta-analysis.'
States purpose: synthesize evidence regarding efficacy of prescribed opioids vs nonopioid analgesics for acute pain relief in ED-discharged patients.
Specifies RCTs investigating efficacy of prescribed opioids for ED-discharged patients as inclusion criteria.
Lists MEDLINE, EMBASE, CINAHL, PsycINFO, CENTRAL, and gray literature databases searched from inception to January 2023.
States two independent reviewers assessed risk of bias, and reports that risk of bias was low for five studies.
States meta-analyses used random-effects model with sensitivity analyses comparing codeine versus other opioids.
Reports six studies with 1161 patients included, with brief characteristics (musculoskeletal pain, adults/children).
Reports SMD 0.12 (95% CI -0.10 to 0.34) for pain, SMD 0.28 for adults, OR 2.64 for adverse events with directions.
Abstract does not explicitly discuss limitations of the evidence such as inconsistency, imprecision, or risk of bias issues.
Provides interpretation that opioids are not more effective than nonopioids, driven by codeine, with implications for future research.
No funding source is mentioned in the abstract.
No registration name or number is provided in the abstract.

title: Intubation Rates following Prehospital Administration of Ketamine for Acute Agitation: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.

items.ai_reason: Title explicitly states 'A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis'.
Background section explains rationale citing prior systematic reviews and gap regarding setting of intubations.
Explicit primary and secondary aims stated regarding rates/settings of intubation and indications/adverse events.
Inclusion and exclusion criteria are clearly listed in the Eligibility Criteria section.
Lists ten databases plus Google Scholar and manual bibliography search, with search date 13/February/2022.
References Burgess et al. prehospital filter and refers to Appendix A for complete search strategy results.
Two authors (CL and AO) independently screened titles/abstracts with kappa reported; third author resolved disagreements; Covidence used.
One author (CL) extracted data into custom table; 10% double-checked by another author (AO); authors contacted for missing data.
Primary outcomes (prehospital and ED intubations) defined; indications and adverse events also specified.
Lists additional variables collected: study setting, methodology, patient numbers, doses, ED disposition, agitation scores, etc.
Newcastle-Ottawa scale used by two independent authors (CL and AO) reaching consensus, with score thresholds defined.
Pooled proportions with 95% confidence intervals specified as the effect measure.
States studies were eligible for synthesis if they included necessary data items and describes inclusion logic.
Describes contacting authors for missing data and Freeman-Tukey double arcsine transformation for data preparation.
Tables and forest plots used; tabulation methods for indications and adverse events explicitly described.
Random-effects model with rationale, DerSimonian-Laird estimator, Clopper-Pearson CIs, I2 for heterogeneity, Stata 13.1 specified.
Subgroup analysis comparing outlier studies versus remaining studies described.
Sensitivity analyses described including alternative back-transformation and leave-one-out meta-analysis.
Authors state publication bias was not assessed because funnel plots are inaccurate for proportional meta-analyses; no alternative method used.
GRADE tool described, performed independently by two authors (CL and AO) with consensus.
PRISMA flow diagram (Figure 1) with numbers identified, screened, full-text reviewed, and included is presented.
Friedman et al. cited as appearing to meet criteria but excluded due to heterogeneous setting; explanation provided.
Table 1 lists each included study with author, year, type, country, and patient numbers.
Risk of bias results reported: three studies high risk, fifteen medium risk, with specific studies identified.
Table 2 presents individual study intubation numbers, percentages, and forest plots show effect estimates with CIs.
Results of synthesis section discusses contributing studies' characteristics and outliers; risk of bias for studies summarized.
Pooled estimates with 95% CIs and I2 heterogeneity values reported for overall, prehospital, and ED intubation rates.
Subgroup analysis comparing outlier studies with remaining studies presented with results and statistical significance.
Leave-one-out sensitivity analysis results reported (no individually influential study).
Authors explicitly state publication bias was not assessed; no reporting bias assessment presented.
GRADE rating reported as 'very low' with explanation of downgrading for inconsistency.
Discussion interprets findings in context of other systematic reviews and ED studies of ketamine.
Limitations of evidence discussed: observational nature, very low quality, dose variation, heterogeneity.
Limitations of review process discussed including ambiguity in indication classification and missing adverse event reporting.
Implications for practice (ED clinical guideline) and future research (RCTs, multi-center studies, qualitative research) discussed.
PROSPERO registration provided with ID CRD42021248447.
Protocol accessible via PROSPERO link provided.
No description of amendments to registration or protocol is provided.
Funding statement declares no specific grant from public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
Disclosure statement declares authors have no competing interests.
States all extracted data are publicly available from respective journal articles in 'Availability of Data' section.
Title explicitly identifies the report as 'A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis'.
Abstract states the review aims to determine the rate and settings of intubation following prehospital ketamine for agitation, plus indications and adverse events.
Inclusion criteria are stated: agitated patients receiving ketamine prehospitally as first-line drug for acute agitation.
Lists multiple databases (PubMed, Scopus, Ovid MEDLINE, Embase, CINAHL, PsycINFO, Cochrane, etc.) and search date 13/February/2022.
Newcastle-Ottawa quality assessment scale is specified for assessing study quality.
States synthesis was completed via meta-analysis with GRADE for certainty assessment.
Reports 18 included studies, 3476 patients, all observational, 15 from USA.
Reports pooled intubation rates with 95% CIs (overall 16% [8-26%], prehospital 1% [0-2%], ED 19% [11-30%]).
Abstract notes wide variation but does not explicitly summarize limitations of the evidence such as risk of bias or imprecision.
Provides interpretation that majority of intubations occurred in ED, not prehospital setting.
No mention of funding source in the abstract.
No registration name or number is provided in the abstract.

title: Analgesic efficacy of nitrous oxide in adults in the emergency department: A meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.

items.ai_reason: Title is not fully shown but keywords mention 'Meta-analysis'; however explicit identification as systematic review in title is not visible in provided text.
Introduction describes pain in ED, conflicting evidence on N2O, and need for an alternative to opioids, providing rationale in context of existing knowledge.
Explicit objectives stated: to obtain precise estimates of analgesic efficacy, adverse effects, satisfaction, and procedure duration of N2O in adults in ED.
Inclusion/exclusion criteria specified: RCTs comparing N2O/O2 to placebo or other analgesics in adults in ED/prehospital; children and non-ED studies excluded.
PubMed, Cochrane Library, Embase, Web of Science, CNKI, Wanfang searched up to August 10, 2021; reference lists also checked.
Search terms presented: (nitrous oxide) or (N2O) (Entonox) and (prehospital) or (emergency...) and (pain) or (analgesia) or (sedation).
Two authors examined articles and agreed on inclusion; data extracted by two independent authors.
Data extracted by two independent authors using Excel 2010; specific data items listed.
Primary outcome (pain score by VAS/NRS, number with pain relief) and secondary outcomes (adverse events, satisfaction, procedure duration) defined.
Variables extracted listed: study author, year, sample size, age, diagnosis, ratio of N2O/O2, control method, pain score, adverse events.
Two authors independently used Cochrane Collaboration's risk of bias tool.
OR with 95% CI for dichotomous data; WMD with 95% CI for continuous data.
Studies grouped by comparator (placebo vs other analgesic methods); comparisons section describes grouping.
Describes conversion of 0-100 mm VAS to 0-10 cm scale and selection of last data point before N2O withdrawal.
Forest plots and tables (Table 1) used to display individual study and synthesis results.
Fixed-effect model used; random-effects if I²>50%; I² for heterogeneity; RevMan 5.3 specified.
No subgroup analysis or meta-regression methods prespecified to explore heterogeneity causes; only mentions sensitivity analysis.
States sensitivity analysis was conducted if statistical or clinical heterogeneity was detected.
No methods described for assessing risk of bias due to missing results/reporting biases (e.g., funnel plot, Egger's test).
No method (e.g., GRADE) described for assessing certainty of evidence.
Flow diagram (Fig. 1) and narrative describing 208 records, 9 additional, 26 full-text reviewed, 14 RCTs included.
Reasons for excluding every identified study reported in S1 Appendix.
Table 1 presents characteristics of each included study with citations.
Risk of bias assessments shown in Fig. 2 with description in text.
Forest plots present individual study effect estimates with confidence intervals for outcomes.
Comparisons section summarizes characteristics of contributing studies; risk of bias mentioned for high-bias studies.
Summary estimates with 95% CI, P-values, and I² presented for all syntheses.
Sensitivity analyses by comparator type (fentanyl vs regional anesthesia) presented as exploration of heterogeneity.
Sensitivity analysis results presented for N2O vs other analgesic methods, separating fentanyl and regional anesthesia comparisons.
No assessment of reporting biases (publication bias) presented.
No certainty of evidence assessment (e.g., GRADE) presented for any outcome.
Discussion interprets results in context of prior studies on N2O mechanisms, comparison studies, and previous reviews.
Limitations section discusses high risk of bias in 7 studies and high heterogeneity of included evidence.
No specific discussion of limitations of the review process itself (e.g., language restrictions, search limitations).
Implications mentioned: N2O as option for analgesia, future RCTs needed comparing N2O to ketamine/propofol.
Registered with PROSPERO, registration number 270963.
Protocol accessible at https://www.crd.york.ac.uk/PROSPERO/.
No description of amendments to the protocol.
Funding from clinical medical science and technology innovation project of Jinan (grant numbers provided).
Declaration of Competing Interest: None.
No statement on availability of data collection forms, extracted data, or analytic code beyond supplementary appendices.
Title explicitly states 'A meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.'
States purpose: 'investigate the analgesic efficacy and safety of N2O in adults in ED.'
Specifies RCTs comparing N2O/O2 to placebo or other analgesic methods for pain in adults in ED or prehospital.
Lists databases (PubMed, Cochrane, Embase, Web of Science, CNKI, Wanfang) with search date August 10, 2021.
No mention of risk of bias assessment methods in the abstract.
Reports pooled analysis with WMD/OR, 95% CI, and I² heterogeneity statistics indicating meta-analysis methods.
States 14 RCTs with 1751 patients included.
Reports outcomes with summary estimates, 95% CIs, P-values, and direction of effect favoring N2O over placebo.
No discussion of limitations of evidence (e.g., risk of bias, inconsistency) in abstract, despite high I² values reported.
Concludes N2O provides better analgesia than placebo and similar to other methods with more side effects.
No funding source mentioned in abstract.
No registration name or number provided in abstract.

title: Efficacy of Low-dose Ketamine for Control of Acute Pain in the Emergency Setting: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials.

items.ai_reason: Title labeled as 'Systematic Review' and abstract identifies it as a systematic review and meta-analysis.
Introduction describes existing knowledge, limitations of opioids, and rationale for evaluating ketamine as alternative.
Explicit objective stated: evaluate role of low-dose ketamine compared to opiates for management of acute pain in emergency setting.
Eligibility criteria specified for design, participants, intervention, comparison, and outcomes.
Lists PubMed Central, EMBASE, MEDLINE, Cochrane Library, ScienceDirect, Google Scholar with dates from inception until November/October 2021.
States search strategy summarized in Supplementary Appendix but full search strategies are not presented in the provided text.
Selection process described but does not specify how many reviewers screened records or whether they worked independently.
States primary investigator entered data and secondary investigator double-checked accuracy using a predefined extraction form.
Outcomes listed: pain score, need for rescue analgesics, and adverse effects (GI, neurological, psychological, cardiopulmonary).
Lists variables extracted: authors, year, study period, design, setting, country, sample size, age, outcomes.
Used Cochrane RoB 2 tool with two independent investigators across five domains.
Specifies SMD with 95% CI for continuous outcomes and RR with 95% CI for dichotomous outcomes.
Studies grouped by outcome and time intervals; eligibility for synthesis based on inclusion criteria.
No description of methods to handle missing summary statistics or data conversions.
States visual representation by forest plot and tabulation of study characteristics.
Random-effects model with inverse variance method described; heterogeneity assessed via chi-square and I²; STATA 14.2 used.
Subgroup analysis and meta-regression described using dose of ketamine and comparison group as covariates.
Sensitivity analysis mentioned in results to assess small-study effects.
Publication bias evaluated using funnel plot and Egger's test with P<0.10 as significant.
GRADE approach described with five components for assessing certainty of evidence.
PRISMA flowchart (Figure 1) described with numbers screened, duplicates removed, and 15 studies included.
Excluded studies are not cited individually with reasons for exclusion.
Table 1 presents characteristics of included studies with author, country, sample size, participants, dose, comparator.
Risk of bias presented in Table 1 for each included study (high or some concerns).
Forest plots (Figures 2-4) display individual study results with effect estimates and confidence intervals.
Each synthesis section briefly summarizes contributing studies; quality and risk of bias discussed.
Pooled SMD/RR with 95% CI and I² heterogeneity statistics presented for each synthesis.
Subgroup analyses by ketamine dose and control group, plus meta-regression results reported.
Sensitivity analysis results reported in supplementary figures showing no change in magnitude/direction.
Funnel plot and Egger's test (P=0.96) reported for the 45-minute pain score outcome.
GRADE quality of evidence stated as 'low' for each outcome assessed.
Discussion interprets findings and compares with previous reviews on ketamine efficacy and mechanism of action.
Limitations section discusses high risk of bias and heterogeneity in included studies.
Discusses inability to perform meta-regression and subgroup analysis by pain type as review limitations.
Conclusion discusses implications for clinical practice and need for further research on ketamine-opioid combinations.
PROSPERO registration number CRD42021289270 provided.
States protocol was registered in PROSPERO but does not explicitly indicate where the full protocol can be accessed.
No description or explanation of amendments to registered protocol provided.
States 'There are no conflicts of interest or sources of funding to declare.'
Conflicts of interest statement explicitly declared with no professional or financial relationships.
No statement on availability of data collection forms, extracted data, analytic code, or other materials.
Title explicitly states 'Systematic Review and Meta-analysis'.
Objective stated: estimate efficacy and safety of low-dose ketamine vs opioids for acute pain in emergency setting.
Inclusion criteria specified: RCTs comparing low-dose ketamine (<0.5 mg/kg) vs opioids for acute pain in emergency setting.
Lists databases (PubMed Central, EMBASE, MEDLINE, Cochrane, ScienceDirect, Google Scholar) and search date (November 2021).
Cochrane risk-of-bias tool was used to assess study quality.
Random-effects meta-analysis with pooled SMD and RR with 95% CIs reported.
15 studies with 1,613 participants; notes half had high risk of bias and US setting.
Pooled SMDs and RRs with 95% CIs reported for pain scores at multiple time points and side effects.
Mentions heterogeneity and poor quality of existing studies as limitations.
Concludes low-dose ketamine may have equivalent/higher efficacy and safety vs opioids, with need for more studies.
No mention of funding source in the abstract.
No registration name or number provided in abstract.
